did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes the SLICE-3D dataset, which was created by extracting skin lesion image crops from 3D Total Body Photographs (3D TBP). The text explicitly states that the imaging system vendor (Canfield Scientific) uses 'proprietary AI-based software that identifies individual lesions on a given 3D capture' to facilitate this extraction. Additionally, the paper discusses the development and use of AI algorithms for skin cancer detection and classification as the primary context for the dataset's utility.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is a 'Data Descriptor' published in Scientific Data, which describes the creation, curation, and release of the novel SLICE-3D dataset containing 400,000 skin lesion images. It details the methodology for extracting images from 3D Total Body Photographs, the ethical approvals, and the dataset structure. This constitutes an original research output (dataset publication) rather than a review of prior literature.

title: The SLICE-3D dataset: 400,000 skin lesion image crops extracted from 3D TBP for skin cancer detection
brief_summary: This study introduces the SLICE-3D dataset, a novel collection of over 400,000 standardized, de-identified skin lesion image crops extracted from 3D Total Body Photographs (3D TBP) to address selection bias in existing dermoscopic datasets. The images were systematically extracted from 1,000+ patients across seven high-risk dermatologic centers on three continents using proprietary AI-based software from Canfield Scientific. The dataset aims to facilitate the development of AI algorithms capable of skin cancer detection using lower-quality, smartphone-like images suitable for triage in primary care or non-clinical settings. By providing a comprehensive representation of individual lesion phenotypes without the typical dermoscopy selection bias, the dataset supports the creation of robust diagnostic tools for underserved populations. The data was curated as part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) and served as the training set for the ISIC 2024 Grand Challenge. This resource circumvents limitations of prior datasets by offering standardized optical resolution comparable to smartphone images while maintaining diagnostic labels.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class; includes melanoma in situ and invasive melanoma coded as melanoma; confirmed via pathology reports/histopathology.
Malignant class; confirmed via pathology reports/histopathology.
Malignant class; confirmed via pathology reports/histopathology.
Benign class; includes melanocytic proliferations (443 lesions) and other benign melanocytic lesions; also referenced in context of 'ugly duckling sign' and nevus confidence score.
Majority of dataset (400,552 lesions); weak-labeled as 'Benign' or 'NOS' if not biopsied/excised; includes various subtypes like fibro-histiocytic, vascular, cysts, and flat melanotic pigmentations.

paper_terms: Adnexal epithelial proliferations - Apocrine or Eccrine
Flat melanotic pigmentations - not melanocytic nevus

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Nevus, Benign NOS).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes the SLICE-3D dataset intended for 'skin cancer detection' and 'diagnostic classification'. It mentions that the dataset served as the training set for the ISIC 2024 Grand Challenge, which focuses on 'AI for diagnostic classification'. The dataset includes labels for malignant, indeterminate, and benign classes, with specific histopathology-confirmed diagnoses for malignant lesions and weak labels for benign ones. The paper also references 'Nevus confidence score' as a classifier output, but the primary task supported by the dataset is multi-class diagnostic classification.

datasets_used.name: SLICE-3D
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 400,000+ standardized, de-identified skin lesion image crops extracted from 3D Total Body Photographs (3D TBP) from seven high-risk dermatologic centers across three continents. It addresses selection bias by including ordinary benign lesions alongside malignant ones, using images comparable in optical resolution to smartphone photos.
classes_used: Malignant (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
Benign (Nevus, Benign NOS, Adnexal epithelial proliferations, Epidermal proliferations, Fibro-histiocytic, Vascular, Cysts, Flat melanotic pigmentations)

use.training: Served as the training set for the ISIC 2024 Grand Challenge.
use.other: Facilitate the development of open-source AI algorithms for skin cancer detection in primary care or non-clinical settings.

datasets_used.name: SLICE-3D Permissive
datasets_used.description: A subset of the SLICE-3D dataset comprising data from four of the seven contributing institutions (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, University of Queensland, University of Athens, Melanoma Institute Australia) released under permissive licenses (CC-BY or CC-0).

use.other: Provided under permissive licensing for broader open-source use.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper is a Data Descriptor describing the creation and curation of the SLICE-3D dataset without performing statistical hypothesis tests.

SLICE-3D.notes: The paper describes the SLICE-3D dataset composition rather than reporting experimental results from trained models. The dataset contains 401,059 total images (tiles) from 1,042 unique patients across seven institutions. The diagnostic distribution is: Malignant (393 lesions, 0.1%), Indeterminate (114 lesions, 0.0%), and Benign (400,552 lesions, 99.9%). The dataset served as the training set for the ISIC 2024 Grand Challenge. Specific quantitative results (e.g., accuracy, AUC) for the challenge are not reported in this data descriptor; the test set results are noted as being beyond the scope of this paper.

SLICE-3D Permissive.notes: This is a subset of SLICE-3D comprising data from four institutions (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, University of Queensland, University of Athens, Melanoma Institute Australia) released under permissive licenses (CC-BY or CC-0). No specific quantitative performance metrics are reported for this subset as it is a data release artifact.

architecture_details.description: The paper describes the SLICE-3D dataset, not a specific AI model architecture developed by the authors. It mentions that the Lesion Visualizer (LV) software uses AI algorithms to automatically locate lesions and estimate measures. It also references a 'convolutional neural network classifier' used to estimate the 'Nevus confidence score', which was trained on approximately 57,000 lesions classified by a dermatologist.
architecture_details.lesion_visualizer: Proprietary AI-based software from Canfield Scientific used for automated lesion detection and measurement estimation.
architecture_details.nevus_confidence_classifier: Convolutional neural network classifier estimating the probability that a lesion is a nevus.

training_details.dataset_usage: The SLICE-3D dataset served as the training set for the ISIC 2024 Grand Challenge.
training_details.nevus_classifier_training: Trained on approximately 57,000 lesions classified and labeled by a dermatologist.

classification_or_output_head.nevus_confidence_score: 0-100 scale (estimated probability of being a nevus)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proprietary AI-based software from Canfield Scientific (Lesion Visualizer) for automated lesion detection and measurement estimation
Convolutional neural network classifier for Nevus confidence score estimation
preprocessing_techniques: Automated lesion cropping: Extraction of 15mm-by-15mm field-of-view tile images centered on lesions from 3D Total Body Photographs using the ISIC2024 Tile Export Tool.
Orthogonal view selection: Selection of the optimal source photo containing the most orthogonal view of the lesion to minimize distortion in the crop.
Manual and automated lesion tagging: Lesions identified via manual tagging by clinicians or automated detection by the Lesion Visualizer (LV) AI software, with coordinates used to guide cropping.
Coordinate merging: Merging of manual tags with automatically detected lesions based on 3D coordinate proximities to ensure each lesion is represented once per capture.
De-identification: Scraping of Protected Health Information (PHI) from metadata files and replacement with coded patient identifiers.
Manual quality control: Manual review of tile images to flag and remove examples of PHI and erroneously identified non-lesions (e.g., knuckles, nipples, belly-buttons, tattoos).
Duplicate detection: Removal of exact duplicates by comparing pixel information across all image pairs.
Near-duplicate removal: Identification and removal of near-duplicates using MONET model embeddings to exclude weak-labeled examples that represented the same lesion as a strong-labeled example.
No image preprocessing: Explicit statement that no image preprocessing (e.g., resizing, normalization, color correction) was performed on the tile images after extraction.
conclusions: The SLICE-3D dataset addresses selection bias inherent in existing dermoscopic datasets by including a comprehensive representation of ordinary benign lesions alongside malignant ones, extracted from 3D Total Body Photographs.
The dataset contains over 400,000 standardized, de-identified skin lesion image crops from 1,000+ patients across seven high-risk dermatologic centers on three continents.

The optical resolution of the extracted image crops is comparable to smartphone images, making the data suitable for developing AI algorithms for use in primary care or non-clinical settings where photos are captured by non-expert physicians or patients.

Algorithms trained on this lower-quality, readily accessible image type have the potential to improve triage for skin cancer detection and benefit populations lacking access to specialized dermatologic care.
The dataset circumvents the limitation of prior datasets that are limited to images captured with handheld dermatoscopes, which restricts algorithm use to skilled clinicians in specialized settings.
The SLICE-3D dataset served as the training set for the ISIC 2024 Grand Challenge, facilitating the development of open-source AI algorithms for skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The dataset addresses selection bias inherent in dermoscopic datasets by including ordinary benign lesions alongside malignant ones, extracted from 3D Total Body Photographs rather than targeted dermoscopy.
The vast majority of lesions (99.9%) are weak-labeled as 'Benign' or 'NOS' because they were not biopsied or excised, limiting the ability to verify benign diagnoses histologically.
Skin tone documentation was not possible due to the retrospective nature of the data collection, creating a potential limitation for assessing algorithm performance across diverse skin types.

Pixel resolution is variable due to varying angles and distances between the patient's skin and the DSLR sensors; users must use relative pixel distance rather than absolute pixel distance for analysis.
The dataset was used as the training set for the ISIC 2024 Grand Challenge, but the test set results are not reported in this paper and are beyond its scope.

The field of view is standardized at 15x15 mm, but lighting modality (cross-polarized vs. non-polarized white light) is a predominant technical factor influencing variability in hue, tint, tone, and shade.
Lesion size measurements (clin_size_long_diam_mm) are approximations derived from an AI model and are less conclusive in the presence of sun-damaged skin.
The imaging system vendor (Canfield Scientific) uses proprietary AI-based software to identify individual lesions, which may introduce vendor-specific biases or limitations in lesion detection.
